reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Governing-majority values: demonstrable equity gain without destabilizing the selective track system; phased, evidence-based reform with pilot validation before national rollout; burden-sharing with non-state maintainers; risk management via multi-year psychometric validation before deployment.
reactions.interest: A governing majority balancing reform credibility (equity gains for low-SES/rural pupils) against political durability (retaining selective-track legitimacy among middle-class voters and maintainers who fund system capacity).
reactions.public_good_frame: Equity of opportunity for high-ability low-SES pupils via admission-rule reform, combined with system stability and voluntary maintainer co-operation.
reactions.argument: S1 addresses the most tractable equity bottleneck — the admission mechanism itself — without dismantling the selective track structure. This preserves political coalition durability. However, the scenario's own evidence labels flag three critical conditions: (1) a workable, low-gaming SES proxy at application time does not yet exist in Hungary (the scenario states this directly as 'contested' and 'assumption — directly contested'); (2) the coaching-resistance of a redesigned assessment is not established by the cited value-added studies (Horn 2013; Kertesi & Kezdi 2013 show the raw gimnazium advantage is mostly selection effect, not that redesign prevents coaching); (3) non-state maintainers' voluntary alignment cannot be assumed — they may absorb displaced selective demand. As a governing-reform voice, I cannot support S1 at scale without pilot evidence on all three. A phased pilot — years 1–3 — with independent evaluation of SES-proxy gaming and coaching resistance, and parallel negotiation with maintainers on rule alignment, is the defensible pathway. Middle-class backlash will occur regardless, but it is manageable if framed as 'access reform for high-ability low-SES pupils' rather than 'quota for SES groups,' and if pilot results show low gaming and real equity gain.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would firm to strong if: (a) year 1–2 pilot data show the SES proxy has gaming rates below 10% and measurably correlates with family income; (b) psychometric validation shows the redesigned assessment's coaching-resistance is equivalent to or better than the current exam on a randomized coaching study; (c) church/foundation maintainers commit in writing to equivalent admission rules for their selective places (not just state-maintained); (d) outreach campaigns in year 1–2 raise low-SES/Roma application rates to selective places by at least 20% in pilot settlements. Opposition would harden if any of these fail, or if the SES proxy proves gameable or unavailable at application time — at which point S1 becomes a rule change without an equity mechanism, and I must oppose it on durability grounds (rule changes without visible equity gain invite reversal).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Governing-majority values: avoid structural reform absent a durable coalition; manage capacity absorption risk; prevent residual-skew entrenchment that reduces system legitimacy.
reactions.interest: A governing majority that prioritises system stability and political durability over incremental structural change.
reactions.public_good_frame: Preserving the selective-track system while improving common-track peer composition through absorption and support, not attrition of seats.
reactions.argument: S2 is mechanically sound in theory — capping selective places can delay sorting — but it contains an internal contradiction that makes it governmentally indefensible. The scenario explicitly states that caps on state-maintained schools 'are directly contradicted by this scenario's own political risks and uncertainties': non-state maintainers will expand selective intake to capture displaced demand, defeating the cap. The scenario's own risk section confirms this: 'Non-state maintainers expand selective intake to capture displaced demand, defeating the cap [evidence: moderate — school_network_planning; legal_and_governance on maintainer fragmentation].' As a governing-majority voice, I cannot commit to a decade-long phase-down that depends on non-state cap coverage which I have no legal authority to enforce and which maintainers have financial incentive to ignore. The residual-skew effect is also mechanical and near-certain: fewer state-maintained selective seats + same applicant distribution = even higher SES skew in the remaining places. This tightening of remaining seats, unless paired with an S1-type admission-rule change, will actually worsen the equity appearance of the selective track, delegitimizing it faster and inviting backlash from both equity advocates and middle-class parents. The legislative and transitional-cost burden (medium over a decade = sustained, not short-term) is also heavy for a reform whose equity benefit depends on an assumption the scenario flags as directly contradicted. Teacher-union defection risk is moderate if performance standards are bundled with the cap. Coalition durability across two electoral cycles is medium-confidence at best. S2 becomes defensible only if paired with (a) legal binding of non-state maintainer caps to state-maintained caps (high legal and political lift), and (b) an S1-type admission-rule change applied across all maintainers to prevent residual-skew entrenchment.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would soften to conditional if: (a) the government secures binding legal or contractual cap coverage from all non-state maintainers representing >60% of selective-intake capacity, with defined penalties for breach (not negotiated 'alignment'); (b) an S1-type SES-conscious admission rule is mandated across all maintainers, so the residual selective places do not become even more skewed as they shrink; (c) the government demonstrates multi-cycle coalition durability through a cross-party legislative super-majority on the Nkt. amendment (not just one coalition); (d) pilot data from year 1–2 show that general-school upper-grade peer composition improves measurably after caps take effect, and high-achiever retention in general schools increases by at least 15%. If legal cap-binding and admission-rule change are not secured, I must oppose S2 as a destabilizing reform that will backfire in year 5–7 when non-state expansion negates it and residual-skew backlash emerges.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Governing-majority values: avoid irreversible structural reform in a low-durability coalition context; manage middle-class flight and maintainer defection risk; preserve system capacity and teacher supply.
reactions.interest: A governing majority prioritising political durability and coalition stability in a context where no durable window exists for structural reform and reversal risk is higher than Poland's.
reactions.public_good_frame: System stability and capacity preservation, combined with targeted equity support for disadvantaged schools without dismantling the selective track.
reactions.argument: S3 is the largest-equity-gain scenario in theory but also the largest political and implementation risk, and the scenario's own inputs directly contradict governability. The scenario's evidence_status is 'moderate on the delayed-tracking/equity link, contested on the peer-effect channel, and weak on Hungarian implementation capacity given teacher shortage.' More critically: (1) the political_feasibility input states 'no durable window exists and reversal risk is higher than Poland's,' yet S3 requires surviving two electoral cycles and is irreversible once enacted; (2) the teacher-supply assumption is 'contradicted by implementation_planning's strong teacher-shortage finding'; (3) the equity_impact field flags 'middle-class and church/foundation exit to non-state or private schools' as 'the largest risk,' and notes 'that exit does not harm the exiting pupils, but it can relocate rather than remove sorting, so the net equity gain is bounded by how much displacement occurs — a magnitude the inputs do not resolve.' As a governing-majority voice, I cannot commit Hungary to a decade of comprehensive-school implementation and teacher retraining (high cost, high uncertainty), abolish a legally-entrenched selective structure (irreversible legislative lift), and then watch middle-class exit to private schools and non-state maintainers negate the equity gain while I have no capacity to enforce their compliance. The bundling argument (standardized teacher training + early diagnostic support + delayed tracking) is theoretically sound, but it assumes I can implement all three at scale simultaneously while managing teacher shortages — the scenario itself rates this as weak confidence. Polish precedent (Jakubowski et al. 2016) shows reversal is possible; Hungarian coalitions are weaker and shorter. S3 is a reform for a government with a supermajority, multi-cycle durability, and a fully-staffed teacher supply. This government has none of those.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would soften to conditional only if: (a) the government secures a cross-party legislative super-majority on the Nkt. amendment (at least 2/3 majority, not a simple coalition majority) committing all foreseeable successor coalitions to the comprehensive model; (b) teacher-supply data show that retention and recruitment of teachers for disadvantaged schools has improved by at least 30% year-on-year for two years before S3 rollout begins, meaning the teacher-shortage assumption is demonstrably false; (c) maintainer registry data show that <20% of current selective-track pupils will exit to private/non-state schools under the comprehensive model (pilot-based estimate), so displacement risk is genuinely bounded; (d) pilot results from year 1–4 show that the comprehensive model reduces the SES-outcome gradient by at least 0.2 standard deviations within pilot cohorts, and that this gain persists across all pupil SES quartiles (not just the lowest); (e) the government commits binding multi-year funding for all three bundled elements (teacher training, diagnostic support, facility redesign) via a constitutional fiscal rule, not annual budget cycles. If any of these conditions are not demonstrably met before S3 rollout, I must oppose it as a politically irreversible reform in a fragile coalition context.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Governing-majority values: politically sustainable reform within existing structure; lower transition cost and teacher disruption; avoidable structural reversal risk; demonstrated international evidence (Portugal) with proven durability.
reactions.interest: A governing majority seeking visible equity gains and system legitimacy without irreversible structural change, via targeted support for disadvantaged schools and improved retention.
reactions.public_good_frame: Equity of attainment (not just sorting delay) for disadvantaged pupils via rigorous curriculum standards and targeted funding, combined with system stability and selective-track retention.
reactions.argument: S4 is the most politically durable pathway for this governing context. It preserves the selective-track structure (retaining middle-class and maintainer buy-in), avoids the teacher-retraining and facility-redesign costs of S3, and deploys proven Portuguese mechanisms (TEIP-style funding, curriculum standards, reduced retention clustering) that the OECD has validated. However, the scenario's own evidence flags a critical weakness: 'weak on whether downstream compensation can offset sorting begun at ages 10-12' and 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's age-10/12 selection age; the Portuguese case did not select as early.' This is honest and important. S4 does not solve early sorting; it compensates downstream for its effects. This is legitimate as a governing strategy — it improves disadvantaged pupils' attainment and throughput without dismantling selective tracks — but I must acknowledge that it leaves the sorting mechanism intact. As a reform-realism voice, I can defend S4 on three grounds: (1) it is durable — targeted funding lines can survive coalition changes if embedded in multi-year commitments, unlike structural reversals; (2) it has proven international evidence at scale (Portugal 2006-2015); (3) it is a pragmatic stepping-stone: if S4 measurably narrows the SES-outcome gradient over years 3–5, it builds political credibility for subsequent deeper reform (S1 + S2, or even S3 in a later coalition with stronger durability). The risks are real: targeted funding is 'the first line cut in fiscal consolidation' (a serious durability threat), and teacher shortage in disadvantaged schools undermines delivery. But these are managerial risks, not structural risks. I can live with them if the government commits multi-year funding via a legal mechanism (not annual appropriation).
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would firm to strong if: (a) the government embeds the TEIP-style targeted funding in a multi-year legal commitment (e.g., via a constitutional fiscal rule or a framework law with binding annual minima), not subject to annual budget cuts; (b) pilot results from year 1–3 in disadvantaged schools show that attainment in literacy and numeracy improves by at least 0.15 standard deviations and that grade retention drops by at least 20%, demonstrating that compensation measurably works; (c) high-achiever retention in disadvantaged general schools increases by at least 10% after the first cohort completes the common phase, showing that the school-quality improvement is real; (d) teacher-supply surveys show no net exit from disadvantaged schools during the pilot (baseline for success given shortage context). Opposition would harden if: (a) the government frames S4 as a permanent solution to early sorting rather than a stepping-stone (it will delegitimize the selective track faster by appearing to accept stratification as given); (b) targeted funding is cut by >15% in any single year during the pilot, signalling non-commitment; (c) pilot results show no measurable attainment gain or high-achiever retention decline (evidence that compensation is not working); (d) the government blocks an S1-type admission-rule change in parallel, signalling that it intends S4 to entrench rather than eventually reduce early sorting. Under those failure conditions, S4 becomes an entrenchment strategy, not a stepping-stone, and I must oppose it on durability and equity grounds.
